text: Hi, this is a case of thymectomy. We did it through subsephoid uniportal VATS approach.
The patient is positioned supine on the operating table, with a double-lumen endotracheal tube visible for ventilation during the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon makes a transverse incision on the patient's chest as part of the thymectomy procedure.
The surgeon excises the xiphoid process and places a wound protector in the surgical site.
A 30-degree lens connected to a high-definition camera is introduced to explore the right side of the chest cavity. The surgeon uses suction and a harmonic scalpel to begin removing the fat pad.
The surgeon navigates to the left side of the chest cavity using a thoracoscopic approach, preparing to explore and manipulate structures within the left thoracic region.
The inflated lung is visible within the chest cavity, indicating its expansion during the procedure.
The surgeon opens both pleural cavities and begins dissecting the pericardial fat, carefully separating it from surrounding structures within the chest cavity.
The surgeon observes the mediastinum dropping down behind the sternum, creating a clear space for further surgical manipulation within the chest cavity.
With the mediastinum dropped, the surgeon begins shaving the pericardial fat from the pericardial surface using suction and a harmonic scalpel at the site of the incision.

Using a nodal grasper, the surgeon pushes the fat pad upward from the right side, revealing the phrenic nerve. The dissection begins, maintaining a distance of three to five millimeters from the nerve to avoid damage.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel for both dissection and coagulation, minimizing the number of instruments at the port side. A suction device and a nodal grasper are used to counter-traction, facilitating the procedure.
The surgical field shows clear visualization of the phrenic nerve, with ample space created by the material block, allowing for dissection without the need for an external elevation retractor.

The surgeon moves to the left side of the surgical field, performing similar dissection and tissue manipulation as previously done on the right side, maintaining careful proximity to the phrenic nerve.

The surgeon begins the dissection from the lower part of the surgical field, moving upward, using a harmonic scalpel and suction to carefully separate the pericardial fat from the pericardial surface.
The surgeon attempts to visualize the left phrenic nerve while opening the tissue, ensuring that there is no injury to the nerve during the dissection.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection near the innominate vein, revealing the left phrenic nerve and its accompanying vein. The harmonic device is used to continue dissection until the thymic vein is identified for clipping.
The surgeon divides small vascular branches, which may cause minor bleeding. A sponge on a stick is prepared to apply compression to the area for five to ten minutes, aiding in hemostasis.
The surgeon applies pressure with a sponge stick to control bleeding, then proceeds to address the upper holes before dividing the sinus vein.

Go after them.
The surgeon reaches the sinus vein and applies an interclip on both the proximal and distal sides before dividing the vein using a harmonic scalpel.
The surgeon identifies and prepares to address the second branch of the sinus vein, following previous division of small branches.
The surgeon applies traction with the upper pulls, using a combination of blunt dissection and harmonic dissection to separate tissue layers.
The surgeon extracts the sinus and inspects the surgical site to ensure the presence of four holes, performing hemostasis to confirm there is no bleeding.
A small patch of pleated tissue is visible emerging from the upper holes, likely related to the previous dissection steps.
The surgeon applies an interclip to the sinus vein, securing it before using a harmonic device to divide the vein.
The surgeon irrigates the surgical area using a media slimer, followed by the anesthetist inflating the lung, which is visible as it expands within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon places a small caliber chest tube into the wound after lung inflation, probes the wound, and then removes the chest tube.
